paragraphs.body: In the article, “The Art of Bowling”, by John Kafalas, the author emphasizes the challenge and fascination of bowling. He transcends his view by giving many examples of beautiful moments, challenging ideas, and fascinating outcomes. This article takes the view of a fifty year old bowler, who loves what they do. First, Kafalas declares that in order to be a great bowler, you have to want to be good in order to be good. This idea stuck with me not only in bowling, but in almost every real world situation. Next, Kafalas disputes that you always have to lose something in order to win. Whether it’s time, friendship, or even other games, real winners have come back from losses. In terms of winning, Kafalas presents the idea of losing first because it inspires you to work even harder to win the next time you have an opportunity. Finally, Kafalas argues that the best winners face controversy on the way to victory. In his example, he faces terrible arm and shoulder stiffness, yet he was still able to walk out of the Easter Classic triumphant at 50 years old. This article resonates with me a lot because I love bowling, and it also presents common challenges that people face and how they overcome them. Although Kafalas heavily emphasizes his love for bowling in his article, “The Art of Bowling,” Kafalas tends to lean toward other goals and ideas that resonate to him. Instead, Kafalas emphasizes many lessons he learned along the way. Kafalas himself writes,”It’s not that winning isn’t important, but generally, my best takeaways are from losses,”(Kafalas 7). In other words, Kafalas thinks you have your best wins after your losses. This idea stuck with me not only in bowling, but in almost every real world situation. Next, Kafalas disputes that you always have to lose something in order to win. When he first said this, I felt a wave go through my body because I resonate with it so much. Not only do I relate to this in a bowling sense, but additionally I can apply it to how I currently live my life. To start off, bowling is one of the most soothing hobbies whether you’re with a group of friends or by yourself. Bowling by yourself allows you to lock in and focus on putting the perfect amount of power, spin, and placement on the ball while bowling with friends allows you to take a step back from everything and enjoy yourself with the people you like spending time with. This idea of losing before winning relates to me in such a significant and weird way because my soccer team and I had lost our county final in devastating fashion just 2 weeks prior to our senior bowling seasons start. Although all of us were absolutely heartbroken by the loss, a few of us had one last go at success at the high school varsity level and we weren't gonna let that opportunity go awry. In addition to Kafalas’ first point about winning, he proposes that you have to want to be good in order to be good. After taking a deeper thought into this idea, it has many more sides to it than I expected. While taking a step back from the sports world, we see many people in their fields doing jobs, activities, and performances not because they have to but because they want to. “You’ll get almost as good as you want to! I love that, because it means there’s no limit to how much you can improve, if you just keep raising the bar,” (Kafalas 2). Kafalas in saying this, emphasizes that the only way to improve is to put the work in and to want to improve. Along with his last idea, I relate to this idea a lot because over the past few months I’ve developed a sense of anxiety, whether it's about the future, school, friends at home, or even just having anxiety itself. But in response, it was my best interest to seek the most amount of help as possible in order to make myself a better person. Whether it’s slowing down to take a deep breath, starting a new routine by going to the gym and taking walks, or seeing professionals to talk to about what's going on, I want to be good at shadowing my anxiety and I’m almost as good as I want to be at it.

paragraphs.body: With both of these quotes/ideas in mind, I believe they lead into the big picture, anxiety is something that is part of me but it’s not who I am. This idea relates to the fact that anxiety may be part of your internal self and not your external self. If anxiety was who I am, I’d have a big red alarm flashing above my head that told everyone in the world that I had anxiety. When I relate this idea to losing before you win, I hope to display that the losses are the internal battles that we face when it’s too difficult to express how you're feeling, and your wins being expressing yourself to the people who care about you and understand the situation you’re in. It’s easier to open up to people for some more than others. While I have an easy time expressing my difficulties to others, people who have trouble doing this face so much more struggle. In terms of almost being as good as you want to be at something, the unfortunate truth is that there's no magic pill or medicine that makes anxiety go away for good. Rather, it’s our daily interactions and routines that help us fend off the internal troubles, activate our anxiety killing neurotransmitters, and allow us to live anxiety free lives. Although it was a tough pill to swallow, no pun intended, I’ve been able to understand that my anxiety won't just go away, so I will have to work on different techniques that will allow me to be almost as good as I want to be at making it go away. And to my audience reading this, whether you, your friend, or even a stranger looks like they can use some support, lend them a helping hand, because everyone has anxiety and are almost at their breaking point.

annotations.comment: Great revision! I think, as you look back over this draft, that you'll see how your ideas, your intuition, and your critical discoveries are coming together. See my notes in-text by clicking on the "View Feedback" link through Canvas grades. Note that I've made lots of comments for revision in-text. See my notes on: 1. developing and supporting one clear, central focusing idea. I think it may be about "meditation." 2. avoid the five-paragraph essay structure! You might want to think about who your audience is. 3. word choice/syntax issues highlighted in yellow 4. punctuation/grammar issues noted by a blue line in the margin. 5. other moments of praise, delight, and suggestion! Keep up the good work. Thanks for sharing this essay with me :)
